title: Docs Viewer Core Refactor
summary: Root feature parent and main-owned control plane for extracting and proving an explicit Docs Viewer core.
content_html:
Docs Viewer Core Refactor

Purpose

Extract and prove an explicit Docs Viewer core while keeping the refactor's planning, decisions, delivery state, working notes, and handoffs readable through the stable main Docs Viewer even when the feature worktree does not build or start.

This root-level parent is both the refactor feature router and its development control plane. The Docs Viewer Roadmap owns its scheduling. Standalone Docs Viewer is the subsequent product feature that consumes the extracted core, while its concept currently describes the shared target composition.

Workspace Documents

  • Refactor Working Method — worktree isolation, scope ownership, branch flow, checkpoints, and controlled integration.
  • Delivery Overview — transition-architecture prerequisite, delivery-size contract, integration strategy, and candidate transition areas.
  • Core Definition Analysis Delivery — derive an evidence-backed, adjustable Core Definition Baseline v1 before transition architecture.
  • Testing — preserve durable contracts, classify historical tests, and run evidence at delivery boundaries rather than every internal move.
  • Git — Codex-managed worktree, branch, commit, synchronization, integration, recovery, and closeout mechanics.

Add architecture, delivery, temporary working-note, and handoff documents here only when the next bounded stage needs them. Keep this parent slim and use it to route current work rather than accumulating implementation detail.

Feature Boundary

This feature owns the behavior-preserving extraction of the core, explicit integrated and standalone composition seams, and recomposition of the existing integrated application. Each bounded refactor slice must also assess the constraints it imposes on open Docs Viewer proposals; those proposals are design inputs as well as parallel work. This feature does not own packaging or releasing the standalone application; those belong to Standalone Docs Viewer after the core boundary is executable.

Documentation Ownership

  • Author the canonical refactor workspace documents in the studio scope on main.
  • Read and manage them through the stable Docs Viewer; the feature worktree's Docs Viewer is never a prerequisite for coordination.
  • Bring committed workspace updates from main into the feature branch through the normal main-to-feature integration rhythm.
  • Do not maintain parallel authoritative copies of the same planning document on both branches.
  • Return durable architectural conclusions to the feature concept or the eventual current-system owner; retire temporary notes at delivery closeout.

A local-external scope is appropriate only for private, machine-specific, or disposable material that should not be repository history. Any decision or handoff needed to continue the shared refactor belongs back in this main-owned workspace.

Status

The refactor, working method, delivery overview, and Core Definition Analysis are proposed. The concrete next step is to commit the main-owned planning baseline and activate the analysis. No accepted core definition, transition architecture, implementation delivery, long-lived feature branch, or refactor worktree exists yet.
